coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] already-enabled rebar.toml policy keys will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: already-enabled rebar.toml policy keys

coaching.coaching: Pin down correct CLI subcommands for gate-health cases with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: correct CLI subcommands for gate-health cases

coaching.coaching: Plan how negative-assertion acceptance criteria gaps will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: negative-assertion acceptance criteria gaps

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind concurrency scenario coverage in public lifecycle surface first.
coaching.subject: concurrency scenario coverage in public lifecycle surface

coaching.coaching: Plan how changelog and release-notes verification coverage will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: changelog and release-notes verification coverage

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether rebar.toml's `enforce_plan_material_pins = true` / `require_plan_review_for_close = true` and the CHANGELOG.md / docs/release-notes.md cutover entries were landed by a prerequisite story; if so, update the Implementation section to reflect that the config-enabling and doc-update work is already complete and this story's primary remaining deliverable is the lifecycle test suite.
evidence: rebar.toml:26: enforce_plan_material_pins = true
rebar.toml:30: require_plan_review_for_close = true
CHANGELOG.md:39-42: 'Rebar's own project configuration now enforces plan-review material pins and requires a current execution-phase plan review before close.'
docs/release-notes.md:11-21: '## Project policy cutover — plan-review material pins and close reviews … (ticket 145e-52a9-26e3-4209)'
findings.finding: The plan's core enabling assertion is already-executed: rebar.toml already has `enforce_plan_material_pins = true` (line 26) and `require_plan_review_for_close = true` (line 30), and CHANGELOG.md (lines 39-42) and docs/release-notes.md (lines 11-21) already contain the policy cutover entries for ticket 145e-52a9-26e3-4209. The plan describes enabling and documenting these as future work, but Grep/Read shows all three artefacts already carry the committed state. An implementer following this plan would either produce no-op changes or incorrectly assume the work remains undone.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Enable verify.enforce_plan_material_pins … in rebar.toml') and ('updates CHANGELOG.md plus docs/release-notes.md')
scenarios: An agent implementing this story finds rebar.toml already has both keys at true and the docs already updated — it cannot determine whether the story is pre-complete, partially done, or whether it should re-verify rather than re-enable.
A reviewer accepting the PR diff sees no changes to rebar.toml or the docs and cannot tell whether AC1 ('Rebar enables both policies') was satisfied by prior work or by this story's commits.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Implementation section: 'Note: both keys are already set to true in rebar.toml and the CHANGELOG.md / docs/release-notes.md entries for ticket 145e-52a9-26e3-4209 are already committed. The remaining work for this story is the public-surface lifecycle test scenarios listed above.' Remove or revise the bullet about 'updates CHANGELOG.md plus docs/release-notes.md' to read 'verify CHANGELOG.md and docs/release-notes.md already carry the cutover entries (no update needed).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'real checkout CLI' in the Verification section with the actual command names ('`rebar claim`' for open→in_progress and '`rebar transition … in_progress closed`' for the close cases) to match the existing CLI surface.
evidence: Verification section: 'invoke the real checkout CLI with no `--dry-run` bypass and both policies loaded as `true`'
No 'checkout' command found in src/rebar/_commands/ — commands are 'claim', 'transition', etc.
The four cases described use 'claim' (open→in_progress) and close semantics (in_progress→closed), matching 'claim' and 'transition' subcommands
test_plan_policy_dogfood.py uses `_cli(rebar_repo, 'claim', ...)` and `_cli(rebar_repo, 'transition', ...)` — consistent with actual CLI surface
findings.finding: The Verification section references 'the real checkout CLI' as the mechanism for the four gate-health cases, but no 'checkout' subcommand exists in rebar — the correct commands are 'claim' (open→in_progress) and 'transition' (in_progress→closed). This non-existent term could cause an implementer to search for a CLI command that does not exist.
findings.location: Verification section: 'invoke the real checkout CLI with no `--dry-run` bypass'
scenarios: An implementer reading the Verification section looks for a `rebar checkout` command that does not exist
The JSON verification record is supposed to capture `command` per case — a misleading term here could produce a non-standard command field in the record
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'invoke the real checkout CLI with no `--dry-run` bypass' with 'invoke the real `rebar claim` and `rebar transition` CLI commands with no bypass flags'.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of the five prohibited behaviors in AC8, add a concrete proving command or assertion (e.g. snapshot-diff the ordinary-list output before and after, assert no new migration files are added, verify CLI help text contains no new pin subcommand) so that absence is measurable rather than inferred from a generic test run.
evidence: 'Verify no eager backfill, graph-wide invalidation event, new pin command, list-output change, or stored relation migration occurs.'
'Run make check and the full non-integration test suite.'

ABSENCE: no targeted assertion (e.g. 'assert list output before/after is identical', 'assert no migration file added', 'grep for pin command in CLI help') is specified for any of the five prohibited behaviors.
findings.finding: AC8 ('No eager backfill, graph-wide invalidation event, new pin command, ordinary-list output change, or stored relation migration is introduced') has no concrete proving command. The plan's Verification section says only 'Verify no eager backfill... occurs' and refers to 'make check and the full non-integration test suite', but these generic commands do not specifically produce evidence of absence for each named prohibited behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8; Verification section paragraph 2
scenarios: An executor runs make check, it passes, but a subtle list-output regression slips through because no before/after snapshot assertion exists.
A graph-wide invalidation is inadvertently added; generic tests pass because no test specifically asserts its absence.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a verification step per prohibited behavior, for example: (a) capture `list` output in the fixture and assert byte-for-byte identity with baseline; (b) assert no new DB migration files exist via `git diff --name-only HEAD | grep migrations`; (c) assert the pin subcommand does not appear in CLI help via `cli help | grep -v pin`; (d) assert no 'graph-invalidated' event appears in the event log after a targeted material change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Implementation or Verification section whether 'concurrent review/close changes' is satisfied by the existing monkeypatched locked-recheck test in `test_plan_review_close_gate_lifecycle.py`, or whether a new real-CLI concurrent test (two subprocesses racing) must be added to `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py`; if the latter, specify the mechanism.
evidence: Implementation section: 'Add public-surface lifecycle scenarios for... concurrent review/close changes'
AC7: 'Deleted and archived target handling, concurrency rechecks, and audited force-close are proven through public lifecycle surfaces.'
test_plan_policy_dogfood.py has no test exercising concurrent close attempts or the recheck-under-lock via CLI
test_plan_review_close_gate_lifecycle.py::test_locked_recheck_detects_change_after_precheck uses monkeypatch (not public CLI surfaces), and is in a sibling not the dogfood file
findings.finding: The plan promises to add 'concurrent review/close changes' as a public-surface lifecycle scenario in the dogfood file, but the only concurrency coverage for this concern (the locked pre_status_check recheck) lives in a sibling file using monkeypatching — not in the dogfood file through public CLI surfaces. If the plan intends a genuine public-surface concurrent test (two parallel CLI invocations racing to close the same ticket), the mechanism for exercising this is unspecified, making the deliverable ambiguous.
findings.location: Implementation section / AC7: 'concurrent review/close changes... proven through public lifecycle surfaces'
scenarios: Two parallel CLI processes racing to close the same ticket — the lock-recheck fires but the dogfood test file has no case exercising this path via real subprocesses
A reviewer reading AC7 expects the dogfood file to cover this, but the implementation delivers it only via a monkeypatched seam in a different file

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion explicitly requiring that `CHANGELOG.md` and `docs/release-notes.md` are updated to document the rebar-project policy cutover (mirroring the Implementation's stated deliverable).
evidence: Implementation says: 'updates `CHANGELOG.md` plus `docs/release-notes.md` for the rebar-project policy cutover'
No AC in the Acceptance Criteria section references these documentation files or the release-note/changelog update as a verifiable output.
findings.finding: CHANGELOG.md and docs/release-notes.md updates are described deliverables in the Implementation section with no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'updates `CHANGELOG.md` plus `docs/release-notes.md` for the rebar-project policy cutover'
scenarios: An implementer could omit the changelog/release-notes update and all ACs would still pass, leaving undocumented behavior change in the release artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'CHANGELOG.md and docs/release-notes.md contain an entry describing the rebar-project policy cutover (enforce_plan_material_pins and require_plan_review_for_close set to true), with no corresponding entry added to the reusable library schema defaults.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the isolation mechanism for the gate-health fixture (e.g. name the make target, helper script, or setup command that creates a clean ephemeral tracker seeded with only the required pre-existing state) so any executor reproduces identical baseline conditions.
evidence: 'In an isolated fixture tracker, invoke the real checkout CLI with no --dry-run bypass and both policies loaded as true.'
ABSENCE: no setup command, fixture path, or isolation mechanism (e.g. 'use the test fixture helper', 'run `make fixture-reset`', 'a temp SQLite DB seeded by X') is named.
findings.finding: 'In an isolated fixture tracker' in the Verification section leaves the meaning of 'isolated' unspecified — an executing agent does not know whether this means a fresh ephemeral DB, a named test namespace, a temp-directory fixture, or something else, and the correct setup procedure is not described.
findings.location: Verification section — 'In an isolated fixture tracker, invoke the real checkout CLI...'
scenarios: An executor reuses a shared development tracker that already contains pinned tickets, invalidating the 'legacy unpinned attestation' case.
An executor creates a fixture differently from the project convention, producing different baseline state and a false-negative on case (3).
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence such as: 'Seed the fixture with `make fixture-reset && make fixture-seed-legacy` (or equivalent project convention) to guarantee a clean baseline before the four cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback statement (e.g., 'to revert, set both flags back to `false` in `rebar.toml` and re-run `make check`') so the back-out path is a documented, explicit choice rather than implicit.
evidence: The plan changes existing behavior: operations that previously succeeded (e.g., closing without a plan review) will now be rejected.
T4 requires: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.'
The plan acknowledges legacy compat (AC1) and 'no migration or backfill' but contains no section describing how to revert the policy enablement if it blocks legitimate operations.
findings.finding: Enabling both enforcement policies changes existing behavior for rebar project lifecycle operations, but the plan provides no explicit rollback path or back-out procedure if the enabled policies cause unexpected failures in production.
findings.location: Goal / Implementation: 'Enable verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close in rebar.toml'
scenarios: If an edge-case lifecycle operation is blocked by the newly enabled policies in a way not covered by the test suite, there is no documented procedure for the operator to safely revert while investigation proceeds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rollback note in the Implementation or Verification section: 'Rollback: setting either flag to false in rebar.toml immediately restores prior behavior; no data migration or backfill is required to revert.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that each enforcement-gate rejection (stale-pin-drift, missing-review-for-close) emits a structured log entry or named metric so operators can observe and triage rejection patterns without replaying CLI invocations.
evidence: Case (3): 'stale material-pin drift rejects claim or close with exit 1, leaves status unchanged, and names the canonical stale target plus stale-pin-drift'
Case (4): 'missing or phase-incompatible close-plan review rejects close with exit 1, leaves in_progress unchanged, and instructs the operator to run review-plan'
ABSENCE: no mention of a log line, structured event, metric increment, or alert for enforcement gate rejections beyond CLI stdout/exit code
findings.finding: New enforcement failure points (stale-pin-drift rejection, phase-incompatible close-plan rejection) are not specified to emit any log entry, metric, or structured event that operators can observe and debug outside of CLI exit codes. If these gates fire silently in a CI/automation context, diagnosing recurring rejections requires replaying the CLI invocation rather than querying observability tooling.
findings.location: Verification section — cases (3) and (4); Acceptance Criteria — AC3, AC5, AC7
scenarios: A CI pipeline silently accumulates stale-pin-drift rejections; no dashboard or log query surfaces the pattern until a developer investigates a failing job.
An automated close workflow is blocked by a missing review; without a structured log event the on-call engineer cannot distinguish this from a connectivity failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Verification section: 'Confirm that each gate rejection (stale-pin-drift, missing-review) is reflected in a structured log event with at minimum: gate name, rejected target, story id, and timestamp. Verify the event is queryable in the project's standard log sink.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_PassRunner` can reuse or extend the existing `_GateFake` from `test_plan_review_gate.py`, or whether a shared `_OfflinePassRunner` helper should be promoted to a test utility module, rather than introducing a third isolated FakeRunner subclass.
evidence: `class _PassRunner(FakeRunner)` defined at test_plan_policy_dogfood.py:25 with a full multi-branch `run()` method

`class _GateFake(FakeRunner)` at test_plan_review_gate.py:60–94 already handles the same `plan_review_findings`, `plan_review_verification`, `plan_review_coach` schema dispatch with identical structural logic
`class _GateFake(FakeRunner)` also at test_block_loop_remediation.py:23 — two prior implementations already exist
Three `FakeRunner` subclass definitions each re-implementing the same branch dispatch; Rule-of-Three would suggest promotion to a shared test utility rather than a third copy
findings.finding: `_PassRunner` in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` is a bespoke `FakeRunner` subclass with a hand-rolled multi-branch `run()` dispatcher that duplicates logic already present in `_GateFake` (in `test_plan_review_gate.py` lines 60–94 and `test_block_loop_remediation.py` lines 23+). All three cover the same `plan_review_findings` / `plan_review_verification` / `plan_review_coach` / `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage` schemas. The plan adds a third isolated definition instead of reusing or promoting `_GateFake` to a shared helper.
findings.location: tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_policy_dogfood.py lines 25–60 (`class _PassRunner`); Implementation section ('Add public-surface lifecycle scenarios')
scenarios: Any future scenario-test author copies `_PassRunner` again, creating a fourth divergent copy of the same dispatch table.
A schema change to `plan_review_verification` or `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage` must be fixed in all three isolated subclass definitions rather than one shared location.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `_GateFake` (or a clean equivalent) to a shared `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/_fakes.py` helper and import it in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py`, `test_plan_review_gate.py`, and `test_block_loop_remediation.py`. If the prerequisite-coverage branch (`plan_review_prerequisite_coverage`) in `_PassRunner` is the only genuinely novel schema arm, add it to the shared fake rather than forking the whole class.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale in the Implementation or Goal section explaining WHY isolated-fixture CLI subprocess tests are the right mechanism (e.g., 'CLI subprocess tests with an isolated temp repo provide full-stack coverage through real public interfaces while remaining reproducible and isolated from the live project store').
evidence: Goal section states WHAT to do ('Prove the integrated behavior through public surfaces') but not WHY this mechanism fits over alternatives
No sentence in the plan explains why isolated fixture repos + CLI subprocesses are chosen (e.g., isolation, reproducibility, no live-store pollution)
The plan does explain scope boundaries ('retain false defaults', 'no backfill') but not the test-mechanism choice rationale
findings.finding: The plan states the goal is to 'Prove the integrated behavior through public surfaces' but provides no positive rationale for WHY the chosen approach (isolated fixture repo + CLI subprocess tests) was selected over alternatives such as testing against the live rebar store or pure-library API tests. The rationale sub-criterion requires an affirmative statement of fit, not just an implied one.
findings.location: Goal section and Implementation section (no rationale for approach selection)
scenarios: A future author proposing to migrate dogfood tests to a different mechanism (e.g., in-process library tests) has no documented rationale to consult
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Goal or Implementation section: 'Isolated fixture repos + CLI subprocess tests are chosen because they exercise the full stack through every public interface (CLI, library, config), remain reproducible across environments, and do not pollute the live project store.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'successful unchanged re-review produces no further propagation' (AC #3 and Implementation list) but no test in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` demonstrates this; the A→B→C transitivity test only proves propagation occurs, not that a fresh re-review of B stops C from being invalidated.
findings.location: AC #3: 'An unchanged successful re-review produces no further propagation.' / Implementation list: 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'execution-time self-edit proven through public lifecycle surfaces' (Implementation list) but no test in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` or any lifecycle file covers this scenario.
Two Implementation-listed lifecycle scenarios — 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation' and 'execution-time self-edit' — have no covering test anywhere in the test suite.
findings.location: Implementation section, bullet: 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation; execution-time self-edit'
findings.suggested_fix: In test_plan_policy_dogfood.py, add: (a) a test that calls review_plan twice on the same unchanged ticket and asserts the second call returns PASS and that a downstream dependent's review-plan --status remains exit 0 (no further propagation); (b) a test that edits the ticket description while in_progress (simulating an execution-phase adaptation) and asserts that the existing execution-phase attestation remains valid at close.

findings.finding: Two more scenarios named in the Implementation section and implicitly required by AC3/AC5 — 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation' and 'execution-time self-edit' — have no test functions in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py`. The resign/re-review staleness guard is mechanically correct and unit-covered elsewhere, but the plan commits to proving these behaviors through the dogfood file's public lifecycle surfaces with both rebar policies enabled.
findings.location: Implementation section (listed scenarios) / Acceptance Criteria lines 3 and 5 / tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_policy_dogfood.py

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
